1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean
5	
6	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
7	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
8	
9	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
10	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
11	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
12	\[
13	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
14	  \qquad
15	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
16	\]
17	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
18	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
19	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
20	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
21	
22	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
23	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
24	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
25	% groups)
26	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
27	% SOURCE QUOTE:
28	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
29	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
30	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
31	% abelian groups defined by the formula
32	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
33	% \end{dfn}
34	%  [\dots]
35	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
36	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
37	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
38	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
39	% topologies  by
40	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
41	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
42	%  \end{dfn}"
43	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
44	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
45	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
46	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
47	homomorphisms.}
48	
49	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
50	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
51	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
52	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
53	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
54	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
55	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
56	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
57	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
58	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
59	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
60	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
61	structure. This is the content of
62	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
63	
64	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
65	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
66	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
67	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
68	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
69	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
70	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
71	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
72	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
73	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
74	\begin{enumerate}
75	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
76	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
77	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
78	    isomorphism classes;
79	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
80	    for \(\otimes\);
81	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
82	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
83	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
84	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
85	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
86	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
87	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
88	\end{enumerate}
89	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
90	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
91	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
92	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
93	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
94	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
95	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
96	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
97	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
98	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
99	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
100	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
101	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
102	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
103	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
104	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
105	supplied.
106	
107	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
108	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
109	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
110	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
111	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
112	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
113	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
114	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
115	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
116	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
117	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
118	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
119	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
120	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
121	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
122	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
123	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
124	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
125	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
126	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
127	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
128	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
129	avoids. The
130	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
131	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
132	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
133	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
134	
135	\section{Mathlib API survey}
136	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
137	
138	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
139	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
140	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
141	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
142	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
143	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
144	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
145	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
146	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
147	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
148	source.
149	
150	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
151	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
152	monoidal-category instance
153	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
154	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
155	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
156	\[
157	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
158	\]
159	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct` /
160	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
161	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
162	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).
163	% SOURCE QUOTE:
164	% "noncomputable instance monoidalCategoryStruct :
165	%     MonoidalCategoryStruct (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where
166	%   tensorObj := tensorObj
167	%   whiskerLeft _ _ _ g := tensorHom (𝟙 _) g
168	%   whiskerRight f _ := tensorHom f (𝟙 _)
169	%   ...
170	%   tensorUnit := unit _
171	%   associator M₁ M₂ M₃ := isoMk (fun _ ↦ α_ _ _ _) ...
172	% noncomputable instance monoidalCategory :
173	%     MonoidalCategory (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where ..."
174	\textit{Source: [Mathlib], \texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct} /
175	\texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}, in
176	\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean}, L104--L125.}
177	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
178	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,
179	\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat}\) is exactly the project's
180	\(\mathtt{Sheaf.val}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) carrier (equal by \(\mathtt{rfl}\)),
181	so this monoidal structure applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
182	\(\mathcal{O}_X\) --- no project work. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
183	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
184	
185	\paragraph{(ii) The localization-of-monoidal-categories API.} Mathlib's
186	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Basic} provides the class
187	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) and the construction
188	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\). The class records exactly
189	the whiskering-stability data:
190	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal` and
191	% `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal`, in
192	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean` (L44, L86, L440)
193	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean).
194	% SOURCE QUOTE:
195	% "class IsMonoidal : Prop extends W.IsMultiplicative where
196	%   whiskerLeft (X : C) {Y₁ Y₂ : C} (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (hg : W g) : W (X ◁ g)
197	%   whiskerRight {X₁ X₂ : C} (f : X₁ ⟶ X₂) (hf : W f) (Y : C) : W (f ▷ Y)
198	%  ...
199	% def LocalizedMonoidal (L : C ⥤ D) (W : MorphismProperty C)
200	%     [W.IsMonoidal] [L.IsLocalization W] {unit : D} (_ : L.obj (𝟙_ C) ≅ unit) := D
201	%  ...
202	% noncomputable instance :
203	%     MonoidalCategory (LocalizedMonoidal L W ε) where ..."
204	\textit{Source: [Mathlib], \texttt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal} and
205	\texttt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}, in
206	\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean}, L44--L440.}
207	Given \([\MonoidalCategory\,C]\), \([W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}]\), a localization
208	functor \(L : C \to D\) with \([L.\mathtt{IsLocalization}\,W]\) and an isomorphism
209	\(\varepsilon : L(\mathbf{1}_C) \cong \mathtt{unit}\), the localized category
210	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\,L\,W\,\varepsilon\) is a \(\MonoidalCategory\) with
211	\emph{all} coherence (associator, unitors, pentagon, triangle, naturalities)
212	derived. There is \emph{no} \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) anywhere in the
213	hypothesis chain.
214	
215	\paragraph{(iii) The proof template for $W$-monoidality.} Mathlib's
216	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
217	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
218	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
219	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
220	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:
221	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W`
222	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
223	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
224	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
225	% SOURCE QUOTE:
226	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
227	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
228	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
229	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
230	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
231	%     intro Φ
232	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
233	%     infer_instance)"
234	\textit{Source: [Mathlib],
235	\texttt{ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal\_W}, in
236	\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean}, L48--L57.}
237	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
238	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
239	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
240	\(A\).
241	
242	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
243	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
244	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
245	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
246	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
247	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
248	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
249	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
250	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
251	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
252	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
253	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
254	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
255	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
256	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
257	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
258	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
259	critical path.
260	
261	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
262	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
263	is already present in Mathlib: the file
264	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
265	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
266	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
267	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
268	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
269	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
270	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
271	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
272	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
273	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
274	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
275	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
276	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
277	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
278	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
279	it is retained only to document the superseded route.

284	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
285	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the

289	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
290	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
291	
292	\begin{definition}
293	
294	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
295	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
297	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
298	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
299	  \[
300	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
301	  \]
302	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
303	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
304	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
305	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
306	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
307	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
308	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
309	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
310	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
311	  canonical compatibility
312	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
313	     \;\cong\;
314	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
315	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
316	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
317	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
318	
319	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
320	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
321	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
322	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
323	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
324	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
325	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
326	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
327	\end{definition}
328	
329	\begin{lemma}
330	
331	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
332	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
334	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
335	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
336	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
337	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
338	  \[
339	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
340	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
341	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
342	  \]
343	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
344	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
345	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
346	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
347	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
348	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
349	  \[
350	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
351	    \qquad
352	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
353	  \]
354	  the associator
355	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
356	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
357	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
358	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
359	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
360	\end{lemma}
361	
362	\begin{proof}
363	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
364	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
365	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
366	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
367	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
368	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
369	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
370	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
371	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
372	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
373	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
374	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
375	\end{proof}
376	
377	\begin{remark}
378	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
379	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
380	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
381	        lem:whisker_of_W}
382	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
383	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
384	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
385	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
386	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
387	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
388	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
389	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
390	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
391	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
392	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
393	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
394	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
395	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
396	
397	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
398	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
399	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
400	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
401	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
402	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
403	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
404	  fixed-base presheaves in
405	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
406	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
407	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
408	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
409	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
410	  it.
411	
412	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
413	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
414	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
415	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
416	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
417	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
418	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
419	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
420	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
421	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
422	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
423	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
424	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
425	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
426	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
427	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
428	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
429	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
430	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
431	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
432	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
433	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
434	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
435	\end{remark}

437	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
438	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
439	
440	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
441	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
442	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
443	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
444	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
445	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
446	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
447	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
448	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
449	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
450	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
451	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
452	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
453	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
454	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
455	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
456	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
457	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
458	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
459	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
460	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
461	to the off-path route~(e).

463	\begin{lemma}
464	
465	\leanok
466	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
467	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
468	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
469	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

471	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
472	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
473	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
474	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an

476	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
477	  % critical path for the tensor group law.

479	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through

481	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor

486	  \[
487	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
488	      \;\;:=\;\;
489	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
490	  \]

492	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own

494	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of

496	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
497	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.

500	\begin{proof}
501	
502	
503	\leanok
504	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
505	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
506	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
507	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
508	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
509	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
510	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
511	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
512	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
513	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
514	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
515	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
516	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
517	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
518	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
519	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
520	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
521	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
522	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of

534	\begin{lemma}
535	
536	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
537	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
538	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
539	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
540	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
541	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
542	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
543	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
544	  morphism
545	  \[
546	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
547	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
548	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
549	  \]
550	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
551	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
552	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
553	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
554	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
555	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
556	\end{lemma}
557	
558	\begin{proof}
559	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
560	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
561	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
562	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
563	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
564	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
565	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
566	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
567	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
568	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
569	  isomorphism}
570	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
571	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
572	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
573	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
574	  therefore extension of scalars along
575	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
576	
577	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
578	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
579	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
580	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
581	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
582	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
583	  the abstract pullback functor.
584	
585	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
586	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
587	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
588	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
589	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
590	   \;\cong\;
591	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
592	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
593	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
594	  comparison
595	  \[
596	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
597	      \;\longrightarrow\;
598	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
599	      \;\otimes\;
600	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
601	  \]
602	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
603	
604	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
605	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
606	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
607	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
608	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
609	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
610	  % "\begin{lemma}
611	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}

613	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
614	  % \begin{enumerate}

617	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
618	  % \item The functor
619	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
620	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
621	  %  [\dots]
622	  % \end{enumerate}
623	  % \end{lemma}

626	  % of functors, see
627	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
628	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
629	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
630	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
631	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
632	
633	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
634	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
635	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
636	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
637	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
638	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
639	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.

642	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
643	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
644	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as

648	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
649	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
650	  \otimes_S N)\).

653	  \[
654	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
655	  \]
656	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
657	  presheaf-level adjunction

669	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and

1023	\section{Route~(e): the \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal structure}
1024	\label{sec:tensorobj_route_e}
1025	
1026	\emph{Superseded route --- this entire section is off path and is not to be
1027	formalized.} The construction below realizes a \emph{full} symmetric monoidal
1028	category on all of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) by instantiating Mathlib's
1029	localization-of-monoidal-categories API. It was the original plan for supplying the
1030	associator. The relative Picard group law, however, is a structure on
1031	\emph{isomorphism classes} of invertible sheaves and consumes only the
1032	\emph{existence} of the associator, unitor and braiding isomorphisms
1033	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, built by hand as the scheme-theoretic
1034	analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), not off a coherent monoidal
1035	category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)). The associator
1036	is now obtained directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
1037	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
1038	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), which uses \emph{no} \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\),
1039	\emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), and \emph{no} stalk machinery. Accordingly
1040	the whiskering-stability lemmas and the stalk apparatus of this section are
1041	\emph{vestigial} and must not be re-attempted. The material is kept for the historical record only.
1042	
1043	The monoidal structure on the substrate is realized by \emph{instantiating}
1044	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
1045	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}): apply
1046	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) to the already-monoidal
1047	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the
1048	sheafification localizer \(J.W\). The single new input the API requires is the
1049	instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), whose two fields are the
1050	whiskering-stability lemmas below.
1051	
1052	\paragraph{Two-tier strategy.} The group law is assembled by a \emph{two-tier}
1053	strategy, of which this route~(e) is the second tier. The \textbf{primary} tier
1054	builds the commutative group directly on isomorphism classes of \emph{locally
1055	trivial} (invertible) sheaves (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), mirroring
1056	Mathlib's ring-level Picard group \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) /
1057	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). On top of
1058	the locally-trivial-scoped associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1059	\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} (all
1060	either already proved or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled), it needs only the
1061	restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and the
1062	dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}; it uses \emph{no}
1063	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1064	\(\mathtt{Skeleton}\), and in particular never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation
1065	(d.2). The \textbf{fallback} tier is the full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to
1066	\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category on all of \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)
1067	(this section); it is deferred because its load-bearing whiskering field
1068	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, for an arbitrary whisker object, needs
1069	(d.2) --- the stalk commuting with the relative tensor over a \emph{varying} ring, a
1070	Mathlib-absent ingredient --- and is retained only for the full
1071	monoidal generality on arbitrary modules, which the group law does not consume. The
1072	load-bearing local-injectivity half of \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) is thus the sole
1073	remaining open obligation of the \emph{fallback} tier only.
1074	
1075	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
1076	the stalk-linearity packaging supporting the abandoned stalkwise proof of the
1077	whiskering-stability field (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, FALLBACK).
1078	The group law never invokes stalks: the associator is built by direct gluing
1079	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). This block
1080	is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be formalized.
1081	
1082	\begin{lemma}
1083	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1084	
1085	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
1086	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
1087	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1088	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
1089	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
1090	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
1091	  \[
1092	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
1093	  \]
1094	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
1095	  characterised on germs by
1096	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
1097	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
1098	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
1099	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
1100	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
1101	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
1102	
1103	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
1104	  linearity half of ingredient (d.1) of
1105	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- consumed by the
1106	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proof of that lemma. They build on the
1107	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
1108	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
1109	\end{lemma}
1110	
1111	\begin{proof}
1112	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1113	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
1114	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
1115	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
1116	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
1117	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
1118	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
1119	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
1120	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
1121	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
1122	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
1123	\end{proof}
1124	
1125	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
1126	the load-bearing whiskering-stability field of the abandoned
1127	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route to the
1128	associator (route~(e)). The group law on iso-classes consumes only the
1129	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), which
1130	is now built directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
1131	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
1132	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) with no whiskering and no stalks. This lemma ---
1133	including both its PRIMARY and FALLBACK proof routes and the stalk ingredients
1134	(d.1)/(d.2) below --- is therefore off the critical path. Retained for the
1135	historical record only.
1136	
1137	\begin{lemma}
1138	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1139	
1140	\leanok
1141	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1142	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1143	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1144	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1145	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1146	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1147	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1148	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1149	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1150	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1151	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1152	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1153	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1154	  left-whiskered morphism
1155	  \[
1156	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1157	  \]
1158	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1159	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1160	
1161	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1162	  instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the load-bearing
1163	  field of the \emph{full-generality} route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path
1164	  (arbitrary \(F\)). For the relative Picard group law, however, the whisker object
1165	  \(F\) supplied by the sole consumer --- the locally-trivial-scoped associator
1166	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- is always \emph{locally trivial}
1167	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), and in that case local injectivity
1168	  of \(F \triangleleft g\) admits a markedly simpler, stalk-free proof, given as the
1169	  PRIMARY route below. The general stalkwise argument (ingredients (d.1)/(d.2)) is
1170	  retained only as the FALLBACK, required if the full route~(e) monoidal category on
1171	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is later wanted; the locally-trivial
1172	  primary route already suffices for the group law.
1173	\end{lemma}
1174	
1175	\begin{proof}
1176	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1177	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1178	
1179	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1180	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1181	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1182	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1183	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1184	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1185	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1186	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1187	  On such a \(V\):
1188	  \begin{itemize}
1189	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1190	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1191	      \[
1192	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1193	      \]
1194	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1195	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1196	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1197	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1198	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1199	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1200	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1201	  \end{itemize}
1202	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1203	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1204	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1205	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1206	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1207	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1208	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1209	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1210	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1211	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1212	
1213	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1214	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1215	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1216	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1217	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1218	
1219	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1220	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1221	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1222	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1223	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1224	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1225	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1226	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1227	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1228	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1229	  \[
1230	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1231	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1232	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1233	  \]
1234	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1235	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1236	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1237	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1238	
1239	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1240	  pinned commit:
1241	  \begin{enumerate}
1242	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1243	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1244	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1245	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1246	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1247	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1248	      above.
1249	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1250	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1251	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1252	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1253	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1254	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1255	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1256	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1257	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1258	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1259	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1260	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1261	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1262	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1263	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1264	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1265	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1266	  \end{enumerate}
1267	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1268	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1269	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1270	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1271	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1272	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1273	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1274	
1275	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1276	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1277	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1278	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1279	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1280	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1281	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1282	\end{proof}
1283	
1284	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma
1285	supplies the two whiskering-stability fields of the abandoned
1286	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}). The group law
1287	needs no such instance: the associator is built directly by gluing canonical local
1288	isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
1289	This block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be
1290	formalized.
1291	
1292	\begin{lemma}
1293	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1294	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1295	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1296	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1297	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1298	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1299	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1300	  \(J.W\):
1301	  \[
1302	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1303	    \qquad
1304	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1305	  \]
1306	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1307	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1308	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1309	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1310	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1311	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1312	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1313	\end{lemma}
1314	
1315	\begin{proof}
1316	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1317	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1318	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1319	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1320	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1321	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1322	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1323	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1324	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1325	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1326	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1327	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1328	\end{proof}
1329	
1330	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
1331	the route~(e) target: a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on all of
1332	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\). It is not
1333	needed. The group law consumes only the existence of the associator, unitor and
1334	braiding isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, assembled by hand
1335	rather than off a coherent monoidal category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)), and the
1336	associator is now built directly by
1337	gluing (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). This
1338	block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be formalized.
1339	
1340	\begin{lemma}
1341	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1342	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1343	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1344	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1345	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1346	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1347	  instance
1348	  \[
1349	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1350	  \]
1351	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1352	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1353	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1354	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1355	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category

1622	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module infrastructure presently available: its construction

1624	  \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\), an object that does not yet

1630	  is built. The construction of the required internal hom, its dual, and its
1631	  evaluation is decomposed into individually-formalizable sub-steps in
1632	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} (\cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} through
1633	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}); the present lemma is realized once those land,
1634	  and its Lean body is a placeholder until then.

1636	  We record the intended mathematical route, all three steps of which are correct
1637	  modulo the missing object. \emph{The route is as follows.} Take
1638	  \(L^{-1} := \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) to be the
1639	  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, and let
1640	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) be the evaluation of
1641	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval}.

1643	  \emph{Step 1 (\(L^{-1}\) is a line bundle).} Local triviality of \(L\)
1644	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\)) furnishes an open cover \(\{U\}\) of
1645	  \(X\) with trivialisations \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes
1646	  with restriction along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow X\), so on each \(U\)
1647	  \[
1648	    L^{-1}\big|_U \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1649	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1650	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U ,
1651	  \]

1653	  free rank-one module. Hence \(L^{-1}\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an
1654	  object of \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
1655	
1656	  \emph{Step 2 (the contraction is a local isomorphism).} The evaluation/contraction
1657	  morphism
1658	  \[
1659	    \varepsilon_L \;\colon\; L \otimes_X L^{-1}
1660	      \;=\; L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1661	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_X,
1662	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
1663	  \]
1664	  is a morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Restricting to a trivialising
1665	  open \(U\), the restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1666	  commutes \(\otimes_X\) past \((-)|_U\):
1667	  \[
1668	    (L \otimes_X L^{-1})\big|_U \;\cong\; L|_U \otimes_U L^{-1}|_U
1669	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U ,
1670	  \]

1672	  becomes the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\) of
1673	  the free rank-one module with its dual, which is precisely the left unitor
1674	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U (-) \cong (-)\)) and hence an
1675	  isomorphism. Thus \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) is an isomorphism on each member of the cover.
1676	
1677	  \emph{Step 3 (glue to a global isomorphism).} The local trivialisations are induced
1678	  by the open immersions with no choices, so the local contraction isomorphisms
1679	  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) agree on the overlaps \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1680	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the unitor under further restriction. As
1681	  ``being an isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) --- a morphism of sheaves of
1682	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose restriction to every member of an open cover is an
1683	  isomorphism is itself an isomorphism --- the globally-defined \(\varepsilon_L\) is an
1684	  isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Equivalently, the
1685	  local inverses glue (they agree on overlaps for the same reason) to a two-sided
1686	  inverse of \(\varepsilon_L\).

1689	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}}, every line bundle \(L\) has the two-sided tensor
1690	  inverse \(L^{-1}\) under \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes of
1691	  line bundles is an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1692	  The local-triviality of \(L^{-1}\) (Step~1) is \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} and
1693	  the local-iso\(\Rightarrow\)global-iso passage (Step~3) is the already-closed
1694	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the single non-available input is the dual object
1695	  and its evaluation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
1696	\end{proof}

1824	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1825	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}.}
1826	  Let \(R : (\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{op} \to \mathtt{CommRingCat}\) be the structure
1827	  presheaf of a scheme \(X\), and let \(A, B\) be presheaves of
1828	  \(R\)-modules (objects of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ
1829	  \mathtt{forget_2}\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\)). For each point
1830	  \(x \in X\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \((R \circ
1831	  \mathtt{forget_2}).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-modules

1833	    (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1834	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;

1844	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1845	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1846	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1847	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The

1849	  ground ring \(R(U)\) itself colimits to the stalk ring \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\);

1853	      \;\cong\;
1854	    \bigl(\mathrm{colim}_{U \ni x} A(U)\bigr)
1855	      \otimes_{\,\mathrm{colim}_{U \ni x} R(U)\,}
1856	    \bigl(\mathrm{colim}_{U \ni x} B(U)\bigr),
1857	  \]

2189	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
2190	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
2191	
2192	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
2193	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
2194	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
2195	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
2196	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
2197	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
2198	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
2199	
2200	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
2201	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2202	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
2203	\[
2204	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2205	  \;:\equiv\;
2206	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
2207	\]
2208	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
2209	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
2210	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
2211	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
2212	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
2213	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
2214	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2215	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
2216	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).

2509	        lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2510	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}

2512	  existence-of-isomorphism, read as an equality of classes in \(\Pic X\).

2514	  \emph{The operations are well-defined.} The product \([M] \cdot [M'] := [M
2515	  \otimes_X M']\) lands in \(\Pic X\) because \(\otimes_X\) preserves invertibility
2516	  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_tensor}), and it is independent of representatives by the
2517	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2518	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}): an isomorphism in either
2519	  argument tensors to an isomorphism, hence an equality of classes. The unit
2520	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\) lies in \(\Pic X\) by \cref{lem:isinvertible_unit}. The
2521	  inverse \([M] \mapsto [N]\), \(N\) any membership witness of
2522	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\), is well-defined by
2523	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef} --- the witness is determined up to
2524	  isomorphism, so its class depends only on \([M]\). Crucially, \emph{no inverse
2525	  object is constructed}: the inverse is read off the existential carried by
2526	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\).
2527	
2528	  \emph{The axioms.} Each axiom is the image, under passage to iso-classes, of one
2529	  coherence isomorphism of \(\otimes_X\):
2530	  \[
2531	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle \lambda \rangle,
2532	    \quad
2533	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle \rho \rangle,
2534	    \quad
2535	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle \alpha \rangle,
2536	    \quad
2537	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle \beta \rangle,
2538	    \quad
2539	    \mathtt{mul\_left\_inv} \leftarrow \langle \varepsilon \rangle,
2540	  \]
2541	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity
2542	  is the invertible-case associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible},
2543	  commutativity is the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, and the left-inverse
2544	  law \([N] \cdot [M] = [N \otimes_X M] = [\mathcal{O}_X] = 1\) is the witness
2545	  isomorphism \(\varepsilon : N \otimes_X M \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) itself (obtained
2546	  from \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the braiding). Each axiom asserts
2547	  only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\), so no pentagon, triangle, or
2548	  hexagon coherence is invoked; commutativity makes the left-inverse law suffice
2549	  for a two-sided inverse.

2551	  This is the invertibility-carrier analogue of
2552	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, differing in exactly one respect: the
2553	  left-inverse field is discharged by the predicate's own membership witness rather
2554	  than by the manufactured dual of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The
2555	  whole dual-infrastructure obligation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} is
2556	  thereby off this group's critical path.
2557	\end{proof}

2648	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of

\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
the stalk-linearity packaging supporting the abandoned stalkwise proof of the
whiskering-stability field (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, FALLBACK).
The group law never invokes stalks: the associator is built by direct gluing
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). This block
is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be formalized.

\begin{lemma}
% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.

[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
  \[
    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
  \]
  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
  characterised on germs by
  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).

  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
  linearity half of ingredient (d.1) of
  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- consumed by the
  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proof of that lemma. They build on the
  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.

`\uses{}` of both `lem:stalk_tensor_commutation` and
`lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk` — both live lemmas in the new d.2
section (`sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor`). The "must not be formalized" label is stale.

**Fix.** Replaced with accurate description identifying the lemma as a live d.1
ingredient consumed by the d.2 route. Also updated the `% SUPERSEDED route …`
comment inside the lemma and the body text (lines 1103–1108) to reflect that the
four declarations are consumed by `lem:stalk_tensor_commutation` and
`lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk`, not by `lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W`.

**Problem.** The lemma body said:
> "the live whiskering-stability data for J.W.IsMonoidal (route~(e)) are the
> flatness-free variants W_whiskerLeft_of_W / W_whiskerRight_of_W (lem:whisker_of_W)
> … superseded on the critical path by the _of_W variants of lem:whisker_of_W."

With this iter's changes, `lem:whisker_of_W` is NOT the live superseder; the actual
live route closes the whiskering obligation via the d.2 stalk–tensor commutation
`lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk`.

**Fix.** Replaced with: "the live whiskering-stability obligation for the unconditional
associator is closed for an arbitrary whiskering object F by the stalk–tensor
commutation lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk (sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor)
… superseded on the critical path by the d.2 route of sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor."

**Problem.** The "lower-risk path" paragraph said:
> "The only remaining obligations … lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso (which the primary

> lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible. In particular the group law never invokes the

**Contradiction:** the opening paragraph says `lem:whisker_of_W` and

The section header and intro (lines 1026–1041) say:

> "Accordingly the whiskering-stability lemmas and the stalk apparatus of this section

The section statement needs to be qualified: it is the *LocalizedMonoidal /
JW.IsMonoidal* apparatus that is vestigial, not all stalk machinery.

### Flag C — `sec:tensorobj_motivation` (line 133): "stalk apparatus is vestigial"

The motivation section says:
> "The whole LocalizedMonoidal / J.W.IsMonoidal / stalk apparatus is therefore
> vestigial for the group law and off the critical path."

The "stalk apparatus" part is now partially false: the d.2 stalk–tensor commutation
IS on the critical path for the associator (which is on the critical path for the
group law). The sentence should be qualified to refer only to the
LocalizedMonoidal/JW.IsMonoidal portion as vestigial.

`lem:stalk_linear_map` is physically located inside `sec:tensorobj_route_e`

ingredient of the d.2 route. Its placement gives a misleading signal to the prover
that the lemma need not be formalized. Consider moving it to
